summary: Joel Spolsky provides an elaborate, Talmud-style commentary on a blog post by tech pundit Steve Gillmor, annotating every obscure reference and name-drop to make it comprehensible. Spolsky explains Gillmor's criticisms of Sun CEO Jonathan Schwartz's advocacy for 'thick clients' and Dave Winer's commentary on JSON and web APIs. Through three hours of research and annotation, Spolsky demonstrates that Gillmor's central arguments collapse under scrutiny: Schwartz was actually arguing for Ajax-style rich clients (which Gillmor also favors), and Winer's JSON discussion was a simple misunderstanding he'd already corrected. The post serves as both a public service translation and an implicit critique of punditry that relies on impenetrable insider references and fails to verify its own premises.
key_points: Steve Gillmor's writing is nearly incomprehensible due to unexplained insider references, name-drops, and jargon that require extensive annotation to parse
Gillmor's critique of Jonathan Schwartz's 'thick client' advocacy is based on a misreading — Schwartz was actually arguing for Ajax/JavaScript as 'rich clients,' a position Gillmor himself holds
Tech pundits frequently extrapolate wildly about nascent trends as if they've already conquered the world, like assuming porting Microsoft Office to the web is just 'flipping the switch'
PR firms attempting to infiltrate the blogosphere with traditional press release tactics are training bloggers' spam filters and generating ill-will toward their clients
After three hours of careful research, Gillmor's entire thesis about old dogs needing new tricks 'completely collapses in a puff of smoke'
money_quotes: Nobody I know can understand a thing Steve Gillmor is talking about, mainly because he makes so many obscure references without explaining them.
It took me over three hours to research and explain all this, and, as you'll see if you follow closely, Gillmor's entire argument fell apart under scrutiny.
One of the frequent failings of pundits is a strong tendency to extrapolate wildly about trends which haven't even begun yet, and assume that they will take over the world.
There was no recognition that web-based word processors and spreadsheets, while amusing, have merely a sliver of the capabilities of Windows-based apps.

These releases are sent by completely clueless PR professionals who have absolutely no idea what's going on; not only are they clueless, but they actually think they're clever by adding a few bloggers to their spam directories.
What they're really doing is training bloggers' bayesian spam filters to ignore the company they're supposedly promoting, and generating ill-will towards their clients by spamming on their behalf.
Gillmor's saber rattling is endearing but completely impotent.
key_insight: Punditry that relies on impenetrable jargon and insider references often collapses entirely when someone takes the time to actually research and verify its claims.
